albums.title: Discovery
albums.reason: Digital Love, Superheroes, Aerodynamic, Crescendolls — you've played these tracks hundreds of times, but the full album as a single listen is a different experience entirely. Time to go back to the vault.

albums.title: In Rainbows
albums.reason: Reckoner is your most-played Radiohead track by a mile, and it lives here. This album hits differently as a full sit-down listen — it's the warmer, more intimate side of a band you've given 2050 plays to.

albums.title: The Dark Side of the Moon
albums.reason: You've given Pink Floyd nearly 1200 plays across your lifetime — this is the one album that rewards the full uninterrupted listen, and it's probably been a while since you gave it that.

albums.title: Moon Safari
albums.reason: With over 1000 plays on Air, Moon Safari is the cornerstone — it's the album that defined their sound and still holds up as one of the most perfectly sculpted French electronic records ever made.

albums.title: Melody A.M.
albums.reason: Over 1000 plays on Röyksopp and this debut is still the peak — glacial, warm, and achingly beautiful. A perfect comfort revisit from the vault.

albums.title: Leftism
albums.reason: Release the Pressure is one of your top all-time tracks at 120 plays and it lives right here. Leftism as a whole is a landmark — a record that rewards rediscovery at volume.

albums.title: In Sides
albums.reason: You've given Orbital 777 plays and this is their most ambitious album — a double LP of cinematic electronic depth that goes far beyond the dancefloor. Worth a proper re-listen.

albums.title: James Blake
albums.reason: CMYK has 90 plays and Postpone sits at 156 — your most-played track of all time. Both are on this debut, which is still his most raw and extraordinary record. Pull it back out.

albums.title: II
albums.reason: You love Radiohead's emotional weight and Daft Punk's electronic precision — Moderat sits exactly in that space, blending icy Berlin techno with deeply felt songwriting. II is their masterpiece.

albums.title: Music Has the Right to Children
albums.reason: Given your deep love for Air, Röyksopp, and Daft Punk's ambient side like Veridis Quo, Boards of Canada are a natural next step — this album is one of the most beloved cult records in electronic music.

albums.title: Black Sands
albums.reason: You liked Kiara, which is from this album — Black Sands is where Bonobo found his voice, weaving live instruments into downtempo electronic music in a way that echoes your love of Air and Röyksopp.

albums.title: Mezzanine
albums.reason: You have 22 recent plays on Massive Attack, but this album sits in a different league — it's their darkest, most cinematic record and shares the brooding weight you love in Radiohead and Pink Floyd.

albums.title: The Last Resort
albums.reason: If you love the atmospheric side of Justice and the melodic precision of Daft Punk, Trentemøller's debut is a revelation — a cult classic of dark, cinematic Scandinavian electronic music that deserves your ears.

albums.title: Rounds
albums.reason: You have 31 recent plays on Four Tet but Rounds is the place to go deeper — it's his most celebrated album, and its blend of folk samples, jazz textures, and electronic warmth connects your all-time taste dots perfectly.

albums.title: Immunity
albums.reason: Your love of Orbital's architecture, Leftfield's drive, and Underworld's intensity all point here — Immunity is a modern classic of electronic album-making, as emotional as it is physical.

albums.title: Supermodified
albums.reason: You love the production craft of Daft Punk and Justice — Amon Tobin's Supermodified takes jazz samples and rebuilds them into something entirely alien and hypnotic. A cult classic that fits your ear perfectly.

albums.title: Corollaries
albums.reason: Your recent deep listening to Hania Rani, Nils Frahm, and Martin Kohlstedt puts you squarely in the world of expressive contemporary piano — Melnyk's continuous technique is unlike anything else and just as immersive.

albums.title: Knee-Deep in the North Sea
albums.reason: You've been exploring jazz via Oscar Peterson, Bill Evans, and the ambient-adjacent side via Brambles and Ólafur Arnalds — Portico Quartet live beautifully at that intersection, with hang drums and hushed melodies.

albums.title: Elaenia
albums.reason: Your current arc of DJ Koze, Weval, and Four Tet all point toward music that's electronic but emotionally warm — Elaenia is exactly that, a gorgeous debut that sounds like jazz and dance music dreaming together.

albums.title: At the Pershing: But Not for Me
albums.reason: You've been living inside the Oscar Peterson Trio and Bill Evans this year — Ahmad Jamal's live Pershing record is another piano trio pinnacle, all space and swing, that sits perfectly in that current pull.

albums.title: Con Todo El Mundo
albums.reason: Your recent love of Bill Withers, Charles Bradley, Sault, and Aroop Roy shows a strong pull toward soulful, groove-rooted music — Khruangbin's hazy blend of soul, psych, and global rhythm is a natural next chapter.

albums.title: Designer
albums.reason: The quiet introspective quality you've been seeking in Brambles, Ólafur Arnalds, and Hania Rani has a vocal equivalent in Aldous Harding — this album is delicate, strange, and deeply compelling.

albums.title: IV
albums.reason: Your interest in jazz via Oscar Peterson and Bill Evans alongside your love of electronic texture in DJ Koze and Four Tet makes BBNG's IV a perfect bridge — live jazz with hip-hop DNA and serious compositional weight.

albums.title: Persona
albums.reason: With Max Cooper, Dominik Eulberg, and Apparat in heavy rotation, you're clearly drawn to cerebral, emotionally resonant electronic music — Rival Consoles' Persona is exactly that, a modern classic of modular-tinged feeling.

albums.title: Prehension
albums.reason: You've played 15 tracks by Joep Beving in the last 7 days — Prehension is his debut and the place where his quietly devastating solo piano sound first fully arrived. A natural companion to Liminal.

albums.title: Waltz for Debby
albums.reason: You've been deep inside Bill Evans this month — Waltz for Debby is arguably his most beloved live recording, captured at the Village Vanguard with a warmth and presence that feels exactly right for where you are now.

albums.title: We Get Requests
albums.reason: You've given the Oscar Peterson Trio 36 plays this month on Night Train — We Get Requests is the natural companion, more relaxed and intimate, and one of the most beautiful piano trio records ever made.

albums.title: Solo
albums.reason: You've been in a Nils Frahm moment this month with multiple albums in play — Solo is his most stripped-back and personal record, just him and a small upright piano, and it fits your current quieter mood perfectly.

albums.title: Geography
albums.reason: You had Tom Misch's Full Circle in heavy rotation recently — Geography is where his sound fully bloomed, a beautifully crafted record of jazzy guitar-led soul that's even more expansive and rewards deep listening.

albums.title: Starfruit
albums.reason: You've been playing Moonchild's Voyager this month — Starfruit is their earlier and arguably richer album, with the same hushed jazzy neo-soul but more adventurous arrangements. A perfect companion.

albums.title: Carboot Soul
albums.reason: You've had Nightmares on Wax on recently and your current weekly listening is full of late-night jazz and groove — Carboot Soul is their warmest, most beloved album and it fits the vibe of your recent tracks perfectly.

albums.title: Traces
albums.reason: Electric Intervals has been in your recent rotation and your current week is all about delicate, textured music — Traces is Heyne's companion piece, equally beautiful and perfectly tuned to this introspective moment.

songs.title: Harder, Better, Faster, Stronger
songs.reason: You've played Discovery-era Daft Punk tracks hundreds of times but this one is a cornerstone — pure robotic joy and one of the defining electronic tracks of its era. It's been in the vault long enough.

songs.title: All I Need
songs.reason: Reckoner is your most-played Radiohead track, and All I Need lives on the same In Rainbows album — it's arguably even more intense, building from a whisper to a crushing wave. A track that deserves more of your plays.

songs.title: Remind Me
songs.reason: With 1062 plays on Röyksopp, this is one of their most iconic and emotionally resonant tracks — a perfect slice of Nordic melancholy and warmth that you may not have put on in a while.

songs.title: Cowgirl
songs.reason: Born Slippy sits at 88 plays for you, but Cowgirl is the deeper cut from the same Dubnobasswithmyheadman era — a slow-burning, transcendent rave track that rewards patience and volume.

songs.title: Last Nite
songs.reason: 652 plays on The Strokes — Last Nite is the song that captured everything urgent and effortlessly cool about Is This It, and it's the kind of track that hits differently when you haven't played it in a season.

songs.title: D.A.N.C.E.
songs.reason: With 771 plays on Justice, D.A.N.C.E. is their most joyful and iconic moment — pure dancefloor euphoria wrapped in radio-perfect craft. Pull this one back out and feel it again.

songs.title: Halcyon + On + On
songs.reason: 777 plays on Orbital and this is one of the most loved electronic tracks of the nineties — a slowly unfolding, deeply emotional piece of music that stands alongside anything in the canon. Due for a revisit.

songs.title: Comfortably Numb
songs.reason: Nearly 1200 plays on Pink Floyd across your lifetime and this is the peak of their guitar-driven emotional power — that second solo is one of the greatest moments in rock music, full stop.

songs.title: Bad Kingdom
songs.reason: The emotional tension you love in Radiohead and the electronic craftsmanship of Daft Punk collide in Bad Kingdom — it's Moderat at their most hypnotic and is one of the great electronic songs of the 2010s.

songs.title: Roygbiv
songs.reason: Your love of Daft Punk's ambient moments like Veridis Quo and Nightvision points directly here — Roygbiv is one of the most emotionally affecting short pieces in electronic music, pure nostalgic wonder in two minutes.

songs.title: Moan
songs.reason: Your love of Justice's French filter-house and Leftfield's dark drive finds a Scandinavian cousin in Moan — cinematic, slow-burning, and deeply atmospheric in a way that suits your ear perfectly.

songs.title: Open Eye Signal
songs.reason: If Leftfield's Release the Pressure and Underworld's Cowgirl are in your bloodstream, Open Eye Signal is the modern heir — a 10-minute piece of electronic music that builds to something genuinely overwhelming.

songs.title: Slowly
songs.reason: Your love of crafted, detail-rich electronic music in Daft Punk and Orbital finds something thrillingly different in Slowly — Amon Tobin's use of jazz samples as raw material produces something utterly unique.

songs.title: Never Catch Me
songs.reason: Your Kanye West and James Blake plays suggest you're open to hip-hop with emotional depth and sonic ambition — Never Catch Me featuring Kendrick Lamar is one of the great modern fusions of jazz, soul, and rap.

songs.title: Archangel
songs.reason: The emotional fragility you love in James Blake and the atmospheric depth of Leftfield both point toward Burial — Archangel is his most beloved single track, one of the most haunting pieces of electronic music ever made.

songs.title: Sun
songs.reason: Your love of Daft Punk's euphoric side and Röyksopp's warmth finds a psychedelic cousin in Caribou — Sun is one of the great feel-good electronic tracks of the last decade, all shimmer and release.

songs.title: LesAlpx
songs.reason: Your current arc of DJ Koze, Weval, and Four Tet is all about electronic music with emotional intelligence — LesAlpx from Elaenia is the moment Floating Points fully arrives, urgent and beautiful at once.

songs.title: Ruins
songs.reason: Your recent pull toward Ólafur Arnalds, Brambles, and ambient-adjacent jazz makes Portico Quartet's Ruins a natural next listen — it's the intersection of those two worlds done with rare grace.

songs.title: Maria También
songs.reason: Your love of 9 Lazy 9's rolling groove, Sault's soulful drive, and Aroop Roy's warmth all connect to Khruangbin — María También is their most hypnotic and is one of those tracks that never wants to end.

songs.title: Pockets of Light
songs.reason: You've been in a deep solo piano mode with Hania Rani and Nils Frahm this year — Melnyk's continuous technique creates something more like flowing water than music, and Pockets of Light is the perfect entry point.

songs.title: Untravel
songs.reason: Your regular plays of Max Cooper and Apparat signal a love of electronic music with genuine emotional weight — Untravel is Rival Consoles at his most affecting, a piece that builds with quiet inevitability.

songs.title: Lavender
songs.reason: Featuring Kendrick Lamar, Lavender sits at the exact intersection of your jazz listening this year and your long-term hip-hop taste — a deeply moving piece that sounds like nothing else.

songs.title: Poinciana
songs.reason: You've been deep in Oscar Peterson and Bill Evans piano trio territory this year — Poinciana is the Ahmad Jamal classic that blew everyone away in its time, built on a bass pattern so good it became the foundation for countless samples.

songs.title: The Barrel
songs.reason: The quiet, searching quality you've been finding in Brambles and Hania Rani has a vocal parallel in Aldous Harding — The Barrel is her most hypnotic and strange track, one of those songs you can't quite look away from.

songs.title: Sleeping Lotus
songs.reason: You've played 15 Joep Beving tracks in the last 7 days — Sleeping Lotus is one of his most quietly luminous pieces, and it captures exactly the interior stillness you seem to be seeking right now.

songs.title: Peace Piece
songs.reason: You've been deep in Bill Evans this month and Peace Piece is his most hypnotic solo recording — a single repeating left-hand pattern over which the right hand wanders freely for nearly seven minutes. Extraordinary.

songs.title: Beautiful Escape
songs.reason: You had Full Circle in rotation recently and your current weekly listening is warm and groove-oriented — Beautiful Escape from Geography is his most blissful track, all afternoon light and effortless cool.

songs.title: Says
songs.reason: You've had multiple Nils Frahm albums on this month — Says is the track that introduced him to the widest audience, a slowly evolving piece that builds from a single phrase into something deeply moving. It fits this week perfectly.

songs.title: Maïa
songs.reason: scruscru has 8 plays in your last 7 days and your recent tracks are full of his warm, jazz-inflected deep house — Maïa is one of his most beautiful tracks, sitting right at the heart of the sound you're currently living inside.

songs.title: The Gift of Acceptance
songs.reason: You played Folamour this week and the current track list is full of jazzy, soulful deep house — The Gift of Acceptance is his most celebrated track, a euphoric piano house moment with genuine emotional depth.

songs.title: The List
songs.reason: You had Moonchild's Voyager on recently and The List from Starfruit is one of their most quietly devastating tracks — a whispered vocal over spare jazz chords that fits the introspective current running through your recent listening.

songs.title: Autumn Leaves
songs.reason: You've given Oscar Peterson 36 plays this month — their version of Autumn Leaves from We Get Requests is one of the most perfectly executed piano trio performances ever recorded, and it suits this moment completely.

Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
BENT THE KNEE TO HER VOL. 1
